content: Figure shows variation of Coulomb force $(F)$ acting between two point charges with $\frac{1}{r^2}, r$ being the separation between the two charges $\left(q_1, q_2\right)$ and $\left(q_2\right. q_3$ ). If $q_2$ is positive and least in magnitude, then the magnitudes of $q_1, q_2$ and $q_3$ are such that
explanation:
Given, $q_2$ is positive and least in magnitude.

From Coulomb's Force, $F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$

Now, from the given graph $F$ vs $1 / r^2$, the product of the magnitude of the charges will be the slope the curve.

$$ \begin{aligned} \because & & \tan \theta_1 & >\tan \theta_2 \\ \Rightarrow & & \theta_1 & >\theta_2 \end{aligned} $$

                       [if the given angle is very small]

                       $$q_1 q_2>q_2 q_3 $$

$$ \Rightarrow \quad q_1>q_3 $$

and $q_2$ has least magnitude.

Hence, $q_1>q_3>q_2$

content:
Assertion (A): Equal amount of positive and negative charges are distributed uniformly on two halves of a thin circular ring as shown in figure. The resultant electric field at the centre O of the ring is along OC.

Reason (R): It is so because the net potential at $O$ is not zero.

options.content: If both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is correct explanation of Assertion (A).
If both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
If Assertion (A) is true but Reason (R) is false.
If both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are false.

explanation: Electric field is from positive charge to negative charge, hence the direction of electric field will be along OC. Also, the net potential at O is zero. Hence, assertion is true and reason is false.

content: The magnitude of the electric field due to a point charge object at a distance of $$4.0 \mathrm{~m}$$ is $$9 \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{C}}$$. From the same charged object the electric field of magnitude, $$16 \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{C}}$$ will be at a distance of
explanation:
The magnitude of the electric field due to a point charge is given by:

$$ E = \frac{k \cdot q}{r^2} $$

where:

$ E $ is the electric field,

$ k $ is Coulomb's constant ($8.99 \times 10^9 \, \frac{\mathrm{Nm}^2}{\mathrm{C}^2}$),

$ q $ is the charge,

$ r $ is the distance from the charge.

Given:

The electric field $ E_1 = 9 \, \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{C}} $ at $ r_1 = 4.0 \, \mathrm{m} $.

The electric field $ E_2 = 16 \, \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{C}} $ and we need to find the corresponding distance $ r_2 $.

Using the inverse square law relationship for electric fields from a point charge:

$$ \frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{r_2^2}{r_1^2} $$

Substituting the known values:

$$ \frac{9}{16} = \frac{r_2^2}{(4.0)^2} $$

which simplifies to:

$$ \frac{9}{16} = \frac{r_2^2}{16} $$

By cross-multiplying and solving for $ r_2 $:

$$ 9 \cdot 16 = 16 \cdot r_2^2 $$

$$ 9 = r_2^2 $$

Finally, taking the square root of both sides:

$$ r_2 = \sqrt{9} = 3 \, \mathrm{m} $$

Thus, the distance at which the electric field of magnitude $ 16 \, \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{C}} $ will be:

Option C:

$$3 \, \mathrm{m}$$

content: A point $$P$$ lies at a distance $$x$$ from the mid point of an electric dipole on its axis. The electric potential at point $$\mathrm{P}$$ is proportional to
explanation:
Expression for electric potential due to dipole in given as $$V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p}{x^2}$$

So, $$V \propto \frac{1}{x^2}$$

content: Beams of electrons and protons move parallel to each other in the same direction. They

options.content: force of attraction or repulsion depends upon speed of beams.

explanation: Since, electron and proton has opposite charge and moving in the same direction, So there must be an attractive force between them.

content:
Assertion (A) : Work done in moving a charge around a closed path, in an electric field is always zero.

Reason (R) : Electrostatic force is a conservative force.

options.content: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) and true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
Assertion (A) is false, but Reason (R) is true.

explanation:
Electric field is said to be conservative since, work done in this field is path independent. It depends on the end points only. So, in an electric field, total work done in moving a charge from a point to the next point and again back to the first point is zero.

Hence, assertion and reason both are true and reason explains the assertion.

content: An electric dipole of length $$2 \mathrm{~cm}$$ is placed at an angle of $$30^{\circ}$$ with an electric field $$2 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}$$. If the dipole experiences a torque of $$8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~Nm}$$, the magnitude of either charge of the dipole, is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \theta & =30^{\circ} \\ E & =2 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \tau & =8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{Nm} \\ l & =2 \mathrm{~cm}=2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} \\ \tau & =p E \sin \theta \end{aligned}\\ &[p=q l] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} \therefore & \tau =q l E \sin \theta \\ \text { Or, } & q =\frac{\tau}{l E \sin \theta} \\ \text { Or, } & q =\frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-2} \times 2 \times 10^5 \times \sin 30^{\circ}} \\ \therefore & q =4 \mu \mathrm{C} \end{array}$$

content: A negatively charged object X is repelled by another charged object Y. However an object Z is attracted to object Y. Which of the following is the most possibility for the object Z?
explanation:
In this scenario, we have three objects: X, Y, and Z. Let's analyze the interactions between these objects to determine the most likely nature of object Z.

1. Object X is negatively charged and is repelled by object Y. This means that object Y must also carry a negative charge, because like charges repel each other.

2. Object Z is attracted to object Y. Since object Y is negatively charged (as established above), object Z can either be positively charged (as opposite charges attract) or neutral (since a neutral object can be attracted to a charged object due to polarization effects).

Given these relationships, the most likely possibilities for object Z are that it is either neutrally charged or positively charged. Therefore, the correct option is:

Option C: neutral or positively charged

content: In an experiment three microscopic latex spheres are sprayed into a chamber and became charged with charges +3e, +5e and $$-$$3e respectively. All the three spheres came in contact simultaneously for a moment and got separated. Which one of the following are possible values for the final charge on the spheres?
explanation:
When three spheres are brought in contact the net charge will be $-3 e+3 e+5 e=5 e$ When separated this $5 e$ charge will be distributed.

In option (A), Total charge is 6 e . So, this option is not correct.

In option (B), total charge is $5 e$. So, this option is correct.

In option (C), Total charge is 5 e . But this violates quantization of charge principle. Hence this option is not correct.

In option (D), total charge is -10 e , Hence this option is not correct.

So, (B) is the correct option.

content: An object has charge of 1 C and gains 5.0 $$\times$$ 10<sup>18</sup> electrons. The net charge on the object becomes -
explanation:
To determine the net charge on the object, we need to calculate the total charge change due to the gained electrons and then add this change to the initial charge of the object.

First, recall that the charge of one electron is approximately $$-1.6 \times 10^{-19}$$ coulombs. If the object gains $$5.0 \times 10^{18}$$ electrons, the total charge contributed by these electrons is:

$$ \Delta Q = 5.0 \times 10^{18} \times (-1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \text{ C} $$

Calculating the total charge change:

$$ \Delta Q = -8.0 \times 10^{-1} \text{ C} = -0.80 \text{ C} $$

Since the initial charge of the object was +1 C, the net charge becomes:

$$ Q_{\text{net}} = 1 \text{ C} + (-0.80 \text{ C}) = 0.20 \text{ C} $$

Therefore, the net charge on the object becomes +0.20 C.

Hence, the correct answer is:

Option D: + 0.20 C

content: The electric potential V at any point (x, y, z) is given by V = 3x<sup>2</sup> where x is in metres and V in volts. The electric field at the point (1 m, 0, 2m) is -
explanation:
To find the electric field from the electric potential, we use the relationship between the electric field $\vec{E}$ and the electric potential $V$. The electric field is the negative gradient of the electric potential:

$$\vec{E} = -\nabla V$$

Given the electric potential $V = 3x^2$, we need to compute the partial derivatives with respect to $x$, $y$, and $z$ to find the components of the electric field.

Since $V = 3x^2$, $V$ does not depend on $y$ or $z$. Therefore, the partial derivatives with respect to $y$ and $z$ are zero:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial y} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = 0$$

Now, we find the partial derivative with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial (3x^2)}{\partial x} = 6x$$

Thus, the electric field components are:

$$E_x = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = -6x$$

$$E_y = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial y} = 0$$

$$E_z = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = 0$$

At the point (1 m, 0, 2 m), we substitute $x = 1$, $y = 0$, and $z = 2$ into the expressions for the electric field components:

$$E_x = -6 \cdot 1 = -6 \, \text{V/m}$$

$$E_y = 0 \, \text{V/m}$$

$$E_z = 0 \, \text{V/m}$$

The electric field at the point (1 m, 0, 2 m) is $-6 \, \text{V/m}$ along the x-axis. Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option A: 6 V/m along -x-axis

content: Which of the diagrams correctly represents the electric field between two charged plates if a neutral conductor is placed in between the plates?
explanation: Upper side of the neutral conductor will be negatively charged. Lower side of the neutral conductor will be positively charged. Then the field lines will be from negative to positive as shown in the diagram.

content: The magnitude of electric field due to a point charge 2q, at distance r is E. Then the magnitude of electric field due to a uniformly charged thin spherical shell of radius R with total charge q at a distance $${r \over 2}(r >  > R)$$ will be
explanation:
The electric field due to a point charge is given by Coulomb's law:

$$E = \frac{kQ}{r^2}$$

where:

  • E is the electric field strength
  • k is Coulomb's constant ($8.99 \times 10^9 N m^2/C^2$)
  • Q is the magnitude of the charge
  • r is the distance from the charge

We are given that the electric field due to a point charge 2q at a distance r is E. Therefore:

$$E = \frac{k(2q)}{r^2}$$

Now, we need to find the electric field due to a uniformly charged thin spherical shell of radius R with total charge q at a distance $${r \over 2}(r > > R)$$.

Since r >> R, we can treat the spherical shell as a point charge located at its center. This is because the distance from the observation point to the shell is much larger than the radius of the shell. Therefore, the electric field due to the spherical shell will be the same as the electric field due to a point charge q located at a distance $${r \over 2}$$.

Using Coulomb's law again:

$$E' = \frac{kq}{(r/2)^2} = \frac{4kq}{r^2}$$

Comparing this with the electric field due to the point charge 2q, we see that:

$$E' = 2E$$

Therefore, the magnitude of the electric field due to the uniformly charged thin spherical shell is 2E. So the correct answer is Option C.

content: A square sheet of side 'a' is lying parallel to XY plate at z = a. The electric field in the region is $$\overrightarrow E  = c{z^2}\widehat k$$. The electric flux through the sheet is
explanation:
To determine the electric flux through the square sheet, we need to apply Gauss's law. The electric flux through a surface is given by:

$$\Phi = \int \overrightarrow{E} \cdot d\overrightarrow{A}$$

Where $ \overrightarrow{E} $ is the electric field and $ d\overrightarrow{A} $ is the differential area vector. Since the sheet is lying parallel to the XY plane at $ z = a $, the area vector $ d\overrightarrow{A} $ will be in the direction of $ \widehat{k} $.

The electric field in the region is given by:

$$\overrightarrow{E} = c z^2 \widehat{k}$$

At $ z = a $, the electric field $ \overrightarrow{E} $ becomes:

$$\overrightarrow{E} = c a^2 \widehat{k}$$

Since the area vector $ d\overrightarrow{A} $ for the entire sheet is $ a^2 \widehat{k} $, we can compute the flux as follows:

$$\Phi = \overrightarrow{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{A} = (c a^2 \widehat{k}) \cdot (a^2 \widehat{k}) = c a^2 \cdot a^2 = c a^4$$

Thus, the electric flux through the sheet is:

$$\Phi = c a^4$$

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A $ a^4 c $

content: Three charges q, $$-$$q and q0 are placed as shown in figure. The magnitude of the net force on the charge q0 at point O is $$\left[ {k = {1 \over {(4\pi { \in _0})}}} \right]$$
explanation:
Positions of $q_0,-q$ and $q$ are shown.

Both $-q$ and $q$ charges are equidistant from $q_0$.

So, the magnitude of both the forces on $q_0$ will be equal. The angle between the forces will be $90^{\circ}$ as shown in the diagram.

Hence the resultant force

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{\mathrm{F}^2+\mathrm{F}^2+2 \mathrm{~F} \times \mathrm{F} \times \cos 90^{\circ}} \\\\ & =\sqrt{2} \mathrm{~F}=\sqrt{2} \times \frac{k q q_0}{a^2}=\sqrt{2} \times\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\right) \times\left(\frac{q q_0}{a^2}\right) \end{aligned} $$

content: A + 3.0 nC charge Q is initially at rest at a distance of r<sub>1</sub> = 10 cm from a + 5.0 nC charge q fixed at the origin. The charge Q is moved away from q to a new position at r<sub>2</sub> = 15 cm. In this process work done by the field is
explanation:
The work done by the electric field in moving a charge from one point to another is equal to the negative of the change in potential energy of the charge.

The potential energy of a charge Q at a distance r from a charge q is given by:

$$U = \frac{kqQ}{r}$$

where k is Coulomb's constant (8.99 × 109 N·m2/C2).

Therefore, the change in potential energy of the charge Q as it is moved from r1 to r2 is:

$$ \Delta U = U_2 - U_1$$

$$= \frac{kqQ}{r_2} - \frac{kqQ}{r_1}$$

$$= kqQ \left( \frac{1}{r_2} - \frac{1}{r_1} \right)$$

The work done by the electric field is then:

$$W = -\Delta U$$

$$= -kqQ \left( \frac{1}{r_2} - \frac{1}{r_1} \right)$$

Substituting the given values, we get:

$$W = - (8.99 \times 10^9 \text{ N} \cdot \text{m}^2/\text{C}^2)(5.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C})(3.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C}) \left( \frac{1}{0.15 \text{ m}} - \frac{1}{0.10 \text{ m}} \right)$$

$$W = 4.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option D: 4.5 × 10−7 J.

content: Four objects W, X, Y and Z, each with charge +q are held fixed at four points of a square of side d as shown in the figure. Objects X and Z are on the midpoints of the sides of the square. The electrostatic force exerted by object W on object X is F. Then the magnitude of the force exerted by object W on Z is
explanation:
Force on X by W is $F=\frac{k q^2}{(d / 2)^2}=\frac{4 k q^2}{d^2}$

$\begin{aligned} W Z & =\sqrt{d^2+(d / 2)^2} \\\\ & =\sqrt{\frac{5 d^2}{4}}\end{aligned}$

$$ \begin{aligned} \text { Force on }\mathrm{Z} \text { by } \mathrm{W} \text { is } F^{\prime} & =\frac{k q^2}{\frac{5 d^2}{4}} \\\\ & =\frac{4 k q^2}{5 d^2} \\\\ & =\frac{F}{5} \end{aligned} $$

content: Two charges 14 $$\mu$$C and $$-$$4 $$\mu$$C are placed at ($$-$$12 cm, 0, 0) and (12 cm, 0, 0) in an external electric field $$E = \left( {{B \over {{r^2}}}} \right)$$, where B = 1.2 $$\times$$ 10<sup>6</sup> N/(cm<sup>2</sup>) and r is in metres. The electrostatic potential energy of the configuration is
explanation:
The electrostatic potential energy of a system of charges is given by the equation:

$$U = \frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \sum\limits_{i \neq j} \frac{q_i q_j}{r_{ij}}$$

In this problem, we have two charges, 14 $$\mu$$C and $$-$$4 $$\mu$$C, placed at coordinates ($$-$$12 cm, 0, 0) and (12 cm, 0, 0) respectively. We need to convert the distances into meters for consistency in SI units.

$$r_{ij} = 12 \, \text{cm} + 12 \, \text{cm} = 24 \, \text{cm} = 0.24 \, \text{m}$$

Now we can calculate the electrostatic potential energy. The charges need to be in Coulombs:

$$q_1 = 14 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{C}$$


$$q_2 = -4 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{C}$$

Using the formula for the potential energy of a two-charge system and the Coulomb constant $$k = \frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} = 8.99 \times 10^9 \, \text{N} \cdot \text{m}^2/\text{C}^2$$, we have:

$$U = k \frac{q_1 q_2}{r}$$

Substitute the values:

$$U = 8.99 \times 10^9 \times \frac{(14 \times 10^{-6}) \times (-4 \times 10^{-6})}{0.24}$$

Calculate the product of the charges:

$$q_1 q_2 = 14 \times (-4) \times 10^{-12} = -56 \times 10^{-12}$$

Now substitute this into the equation:

$$U = 8.99 \times 10^9 \times \frac{-56 \times 10^{-12}}{0.24}$$

Simplify the expression:

$$U = 8.99 \times 10^9 \times -233.33 \times 10^{-12}$$

Finally, evaluate the expression:

$$U = -2098.67 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{J}$$

Convert to a simpler form:

$$U = -97.9 \, \text{J}$$

Therefore, the electrostatic potential energy of the configuration is:

$$\boxed{-97.9 \, \text{J}}$$

Hence, the correct option is:

Option D.

content:
Assertion (A) : A negative charge in an electric field moves along the direction of the electric field.

Reason (R) : On a negative charge a force acts in the direction of the electric field.

options.content: Both (A) & (R) are true and (R) is correct explanation of (A).
Both (A) & (R) are true and (R) is not correct explanation of (A).
(A) is false and (R) is also false.

explanation:
The correct answer is:

Option D

Assertion (A) : A negative charge in an electric field moves along the direction of the electric field. This statement is false. A negative charge in an electric field actually moves in the opposite direction of the electric field. The electric field direction is defined as the direction in which a positive test charge would move. Thus, a negative charge would experience a force in the direction opposite to the electric field.

Reason (R) : On a negative charge a force acts in the direction of the electric field. This statement is also false. The force on a negative charge in an electric field is opposite to the direction of the electric field. The force exerted on the charge by the electric field is given by Coulomb's law:

$$\vec{F} = q \vec{E}$$

where $ q $ is the charge and $ \vec{E} $ is the electric field. For a negative charge $ q $, the force $ \vec{F} $ will be in the opposite direction of $ \vec{E} $ because the charge $ q $ is negative.

Therefore, both (A) and (R) are false, making Option D the correct choice.

content: Equipotentials at a large distance from a collection of charges whose total sum is not zero are -
explanation:
To understand the nature of equipotentials at a large distance from a collection of charges whose total charge is not zero, we need to delve into the concept of electric potential due to multiple charges.

At a large distance from the charge distribution, the system of charges can be approximated as a point charge whose value is equal to the total charge of the system. This is because, at large distances, higher-order multipole moments (such as dipole or quadrupole moments) contribute much less to the potential compared to the monopole moment (total charge).

For a point charge, the equipotential surfaces are spherical in nature, as they depend only on the distance from the charge. The electric potential $V$ at a distance $r$ from a point charge $Q$ is given by:

$$ V = \frac{1}{4\pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q}{r} $$

This implies that the potential is the same at any point lying on a sphere of radius $r$ centered at the location of the charge. Therefore, as we move farther away from a collection of charges with a non-zero total charge, the equipotential surfaces approach those of a point charge, which are spherical.

Hence, the correct option is:

Option A: spheres

content: Four charges $$-$$q, $$-$$q, +q and +q are placed at the corners of a square of side 2 L is shown in figure. The electric potential at point A midway between the two charges +q and +q is -

(a) (i) Two point charges $5 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $-1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ are placed at points ( $-3 \mathrm{~cm}, 0,0$ ) and ( $3 \mathrm{~cm}, 0,0$ ) respectively. An external electric field $\vec{E}=\frac{A}{r^2} \hat{r}$

where $A=3 \times 10^5 \mathrm{Vm}$ is switched on in the region.

Calculate the change in electrostatic energy of the system due to the electric field.

(ii) A system of two conductors is placed in air and they have net charge of $+80 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $-80 \mu \mathrm{C}$ which causes a potential difference of 16 V between them.

(1) Find the capacitance of the system.

(2) If the air between the capacitor is replaced by a dielectric medium of dielectric constant 3, what will be the potential difference between the two conductors?

(3) If the charges on two conductors are changed to $+160 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $-160 \mu \mathrm{C}$, will the capacitance of the system change?

Give reason for your answer.

OR

(b) (i) Consider three metal spherical shells $A, B$ and $C$, each of radius $R$. Each shell is having a concentric metal ball of radius $R / 10$. The spherical shells $\mathrm{A}, B$ and $C$ are given charges $+6 q,-4 q$ and $14 q$ respectively. Their inner metal balls are also given charges $-2 q$, $+8 q$ and $-10 q$ respectively. Compare the magnitude of the electric fields due to shells $A, B$ and $C$ at a distance $3 R$ from their centres.

(ii) A charge $-6 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is placed at the centre $B$ of a semicircle of radius 5 cm , as shown in the figure. An equal and opposite charge is placed at point D at a distance of 10 cm from B. A charge $+5 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is moved from point ' $C$ ' to point ' $A$ ' along the circumference. Calculate the work done on the charge.
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { (i) Two point charges are given as: }\\ &\begin{aligned}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, q_1 & =5 \mu \mathrm{C}=5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C} \text { at }(-3 \mathrm{~cm}, 0,0) \\ & =(-0.03 \mathrm{~m}, 0,0) \\ q_2 & =-1 \mu \mathrm{C}=-1 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C} \text { at }(3 \mathrm{~cm}, 0,0) \\ & =(0.03 \mathrm{~m}, 0,0) \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

An external electric field is given by:

$$ E=\frac{A}{r^2}, A=3 \times 10^5 \mathrm{Vm} $$

The change in electrostatic energy of a system of charges in an external field is given by:

$$ \Delta U=-\sum q_i E\left(x_i\right) x_i $$

Since, the given electric field is position-dependent:

$$ E(x)=\frac{A}{x^2} $$

For charge $q_1=5 \mu \mathrm{C}$ at $x_1=-0.03 \mathrm{~m}$

$$ \begin{aligned} E_1 & =\frac{A}{x_1^2}=\frac{3 \times 10^5}{(-0.03)^2} \\ & =3.33 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m} \\For \,\,charge\,\,\, q_2 & =-1 \mu \mathrm{C} \text { at } x_2=0.03 \mathrm{~m} \\ E_2 & =\frac{A}{x_2^2}=\frac{3 \times 10^5}{(0.03)^2}=3.33 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { Change in electrostatic energy: } \Delta U=\Delta U_1+\Delta U_2 $$

$$ \begin{gathered} \Delta U=-q_1 E_1 x_1-q_2 E_2 x_2 \\ \Delta U=-\left(5 \times 10^{-6}\right)\left(3.33 \times 10^8\right)(-0.03)-\left(-1 \times 10^{-6}\right) \\ \left(3.33 \times 10^8\right)(0.0 .3)=0.06 \mathrm{~J} \end{gathered} $$

$$ \begin{aligned}(ii) (1)\,\, & C=Q / V \\ & C=\frac{80 \times 10^{-6}}{16}=5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}=5 \mu \mathrm{~F} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned}(2)\,\, & C^{\prime}=K C=3 \times 5 \mu \mathrm{~F}=15 \mu \mathrm{~F} \\ & V^{\prime}=\frac{80 \times 10^{-6}}{15 \times 10^{-6}}=5.33 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

(3) Capacitance depends only on the physical properties (geometry, medium, plate separation), not on charge. Since, there is no change in plate configuration or medium, the capacitance remains $5 \mu \mathrm{~F}$.

Thus, the capacitance does not change.

OR

(i) Three conducting spherical shells $A, B$, and $C$, each of radius $R$, have inner concentric metal balls of radius $R / 10$.

Given charges: Shell A: $+6 q$, Inner Ball A: $-2 q$; Shell B: $-4 q$, Inner Ball B: $+8 q$; Shell C: $+14 q$, Inner Ball C: $-10 q$

Distance where the field is to be calculated: $3 R$.

For a conducting spherical shell, the total charge appears as if it is concentrated at the centre when calculating the field outside the shell. The inner ball induces charges on the inner surface of the shell, but the total charge on the outer shell remains the same. Thus, the net charge on each shell determines the electric field at $3 R$.

Shell A:

Net charge on the shell itself $=+6 q$.

Shell B:

Net charge on the shell itself $=-4 q$.

Shell C:

Net charge on the shell itself $=+14 q$.

Now the electric fields at a distance $3 R$ are given by

$$ \frac{k 6 q}{(3 R)^2}: \frac{k 4 q}{(3 R)^2}: \frac{k 14 q}{(3 R)^2} $$

6:4:14

i.e., $3: 2: 7$

(ii) Electric potential at any point due to a charge $q_{\text {at }}$ distance $r$ :

$$ V=k q / r $$

Since, $C$ and $A$ are at the same distance from $B$ (radius $=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ ), the potential at both points due to $B$ and $D$ is the same.

Thus, the potential difference between C and $A$ is zero, meaning:

$$ W=q \Delta V=0 $$

content:
(a) Four point charges of $1 \mu \mathrm{C},-2 \mu \mathrm{C}, 1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $-2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ are placed at the corners $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D respectively, of a square of side 30 cm . Find the net force acting on a charge of $4 \mu \mathrm{C}$ placed at the centre of the square.

OR

(b) Three point charges, 1 pC each, are kept at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side 10 cm . Find the net electric field at the centroid of triangle.
explanation:
(a) The force of repulsion on charge $4 \mu \mathrm{C}$ due to charge $1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ at A and C are equal and opposite. Hence, they cancel each other.

Similarly, the force of attraction on charge $4 \mu \mathrm{C}$ due to charge $-2 \mu C$ at $B$ and $D$ are equal and opposite. Hence, they also cancel each other. So, the net force acting on the charge at the centre is zero.

OR

(b) The electric field at the centre of triangle due to all the three charges is zero as it is balanced from all the sides.

content:
(a) Define the term 'electric flux' and write its dimensions.

(b) A plane surface, in shape of a square of side 1 cm is placed in an electric field $\vec{E}=\left(100 \frac{\mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{C}}\right) \hat{i}$ such that the unit vector normal to the surface is given by $\hat{n}=0.8 \hat{i}+0.6 \hat{k}$. Find the electric flux through the surface.
explanation:
(a) The total number of electric field lines passing through an area per unit time is defined as electric flux. It is represented by $\phi$.

$$\phi=\vec{E} \cdot \vec{A}$$

Its dimensions are,

$$\begin{aligned} \phi & =\left[M L T^{-3} I^{-1}\right] \cdot\left[L^2\right] \\ \phi & =\left[M L^3 T^{-3} I^{-1}\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{(b)}\quad \vec{E} & =100 \frac{\mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{C}} \hat{i} \\ \text { side } & =1 \mathrm{~cm}=0.01 \mathrm{~m} \\ \text{Surface area,}\quad A & =(0.01)^2=1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2 \end{aligned}$$

Normal unit vector, $\hat{n}=0.8 \hat{i}+0.6 \hat{k}$

Angle, $$\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{0.6}{0.8}\right)=36.87^{\circ}$$

Now,

$$\begin{aligned} & \phi=E A \cos \theta \\ & \phi=100\left(1 \times 10^{-4}\right) \cos 36.87 \\ & \phi=10^{-2} \times 0.8 \\ & \phi=8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{Nm}^2 / \mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$$

content:
(a) (i) Draw equipotential surfaces for an electric dipole.

(ii) Two point charges $q_1$ and $q_2$ are located at $\overrightarrow{r_1}$ and $\vec{r}_2$ respectively in an external electric field $\vec{E}$. Obtain an expression for the potential energy of the system.

(iii) The dipole moment of a molecule is $10^{-30} \mathrm{Cm}$. It is placed in an electric field $\vec{E}$ of $10^5 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$ such that its axis is along the electric field. The direction of $\vec{E}$ is suddenly changed by $60^{\circ}$ at an instant. Find the change in the potential energy of the dipole, at that instant.

OR

(b) (i) A thin spherical shell of radius R has a uniform surface charge density $\sigma$. Using Gauss' law, deduce an expression for electric field (i) outside and (ii) inside the shell.

(ii) Two long straight thin wires AB and CD have linear charge densities $10 \mu \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}$ and $-20 \mu \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}$, respectively. They are kept parallel to each other at a distance 1 m . Find magnitude and direction of the net electric field at a point midway between them.
explanation:
(a) (i)

(ii) Work done is bringing the charge $q_1$ from infinity to the position $r_1$,

$$W_1=q_1 V\left(r_1\right)$$

Work done in bringing the charge $q_2$ to the position $r_2$

$$W_2=q_2 V\left(r_2\right)+\frac{q_1 q_2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_o r_2}$$

Hence, total potential energy of system,

$$\begin{aligned} & U=W_1+W_2 \\ & U=q_2 V\left(r_1\right)+q_2 V\left(r_2\right)+\frac{q_1 q_2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_o r_2} \end{aligned}$$

(iii) Given,

$$\begin{aligned} P & =10^{-30} \mathrm{~cm} \\ E & =10^5 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m} \\ \Delta U & =-P E\left(\cos \theta_2-\cos \theta_1\right) \\ \Delta U & =-10^{-30} \times 10^5\left(\cos 60^{\circ}-\cos 0^{\circ}\right) \\ \Delta U & =-10^{-30} \times 10^5\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) \\ \Delta U & =10^{-25} \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$$

OR

(b) (i) (i) Outside the shell:

Let $\vec{E}$ be the electric field at $P$. The electric field through an elemental surface $\overrightarrow{d S}$ is, $d \phi=\vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}$ Total electric flux through the gaussian surface is

$$\phi=\oint_S E d S=E \oint_S d S=E\left(4 \pi r^2\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Also, } & & \phi & =\frac{q}{\varepsilon_o} \\ \Rightarrow & & E\left(4 \pi r^2\right) & =\frac{q}{\varepsilon_o} \\ \Rightarrow & & E & =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r^2}\quad \text { (for } r>R \text { ) } \end{aligned}$$

(ii) Inside the shell:

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad E\left(4 \pi r^2\right) & =0 \\ E & =0 \end{aligned}$$

(ii) Given,

$$\begin{aligned} \lambda_1 & =10 \mu \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m} \\ \lambda_2 & =-20 \mu \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m} \\ d & =1 \mathrm{~m} \end{aligned}$$

For a point at a distance from linear charge,

$$E=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_o r}$$

Total electric field,

$$\begin{aligned} & E=E_1+E_2 \\ & E=\frac{\lambda_1}{2 \pi \varepsilon_o r}+\frac{\lambda_2}{2 \pi \varepsilon_o r} \\ & E=\frac{2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_o r}\left(\lambda_1+\lambda_2\right) \\ & E=\frac{2 \times 10^9 \times 9}{0.5}\left(10 \times 10^{-6}-20 \times 10^{-6}\right) \\ & E=-36 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-5} \\ & E=-3.6 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$$

The direction of electric field is towards wire CD.

content:
(a) (i) Use Gauss' law to obtain an expression for the electric field due to an infinitely long thin straight wire with uniform linear charge density $$\lambda$$.

(ii) An infinitely long positively charged straight wire has a linear charge density $$\lambda$$. An electron is revolving in a circle with a constant speed $$v$$ such that the wire passes through the centre, and is perpendicular to the plane, of the circle. Find the kinetic energy of the electron in terms of magnitudes of its charge and linear charge density $$\lambda$$ on the wire.

(iii) Draw a graph of kinetic energy as a function of linear charge density $$\lambda$$.

OR

(b) (i) Consider two identical point charges located at points $$(0,0)$$ and $$(a, 0)$$.

(1) Is there a point on the line joining them at which the electric field is zero?

(2) Is there a point on the line joining them at which the electric potential is zero?

Justify your answers for each case.

(ii) State the significance of negative value of electrostatic potential energy of a system of charges.

Three charges are placed at the corners of an equilateral triangle $$A B C$$ of side $$2.0 \mathrm{~m}$$ as shown in figure. Calculate the electric potential energy of the system of three charges.
explanation:
(a) (i) Electric field due to an infinitely long thin straight wire with uniform linear charge density:

Linear charge density $$=\lambda$$

A point $$\mathrm{P}$$ is considered at a distance $$x$$ where the electric field is to be determined.

A cylindrical Gaussian surface of length $$l$$ and radius $$x$$ is considered with the wire as its axis.

Magnitude of electric field at every point the curved surface of the cylinder is same and is directed outward making an angle $$0^0$$ with the direction of area vector.

Electric fields at the two flat surfaces of the cylinder are zero since, the direction of electric field through those surfaces and the direction of area vector are perpendicular.

So, total flux through the curved surface $$=\phi=E \times 2 \pi x l$$

According to Gauss' law, $$\phi=\frac{l \lambda}{\varepsilon_0}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore & E \times 2 \pi x l & =\frac{l \lambda}{\varepsilon_0} \\ & \therefore & E & =\frac{1}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\lambda}{x} \end{aligned}$$

(ii) Electric field produced by an infinitely long straight charged wire,

$$E=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}$$

This field provides the centripetal force to the revolving electron.

or, $$e E=\frac{m v^2}{r}$$

or, $$\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}=\frac{m v^2}{r}$$

or, $$\quad m v^2=\frac{e \lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}$$

$$\therefore$$ Kinetic energy (KE) $$=\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{e \lambda}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}$$

(iii) The required equation,

$$\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{e \lambda}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}$$

It is the equation of a straight line passing through the origin.

OR

(b) (i) (1) Yes, Electric field will be zero at $$\left(\frac{a}{2}, 0\right)$$ point. At this point the magnitudes of both the electric fields are same in magnitude and are oppositely directed.

(2) Electric potential will not be zero at any point since at every point the potential will be the summation of both the potentials and both charges are of similar nature.

(ii) Significance of negative electrostatic potential energy :

Negative electrostatic potential energy of a system of charges means work is to be done against the field to move the charges apart.

Potential energy due to charges at A and B

$$U_1=\frac{k q_1 q_2}{r}$$

Or, $$U_1=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times(-4) \times(4) \times 10^{-12}}{2}$$

$$\therefore \quad U_1=-72 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~J}$$

Potential energy due to charges at $$\mathrm{A}$$ and $$\mathrm{C}$$

$$U_2=\frac{k q_1 q_3}{r}$$

Or, $$\quad U_1=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times(4) \times(2) \times 10^{-12}}{2}$$

$$\therefore \quad U_2=36 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~J}$$

Potential energy due to charges at B and C

$$U_3=\frac{k q_2 q_3}{r}$$

Or, $$U_3=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times(-4) \times(2) \times 10^{-12}}{2}$$

$$\therefore \quad U_3=-36 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~J}$$

Net potential energy of the system of three charges.

$$\begin{aligned} & U =U_1+U_2+U_3 \\ \text { Or, } & U =-72 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~J}+36 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~J}-36 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~J} \\ \therefore & \quad U =-72 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$$

content: Depict the orientation of an electric dipole in (a) stable and (b) unstable equilibrium in an external uniform electric field. Write the potential energy of the dipole in each case.
explanation:
(a) Stable equilibrium: When angle between $$\vec{p}$$ and $$\vec{E}$$ is $$0^0$$.

Potential energy of dipole $$=-p E \cos \theta$$

In this case $$\theta=0^0$$, so Potential energy $$=-p \mathrm{E}$$

(b) Unstable equilibrium: When angle between $$\vec{p}$$ and $$\vec{E}$$ is $$180^{\circ}$$.

Potential energy of dipole $$=-p E \cos \theta$$

In this case $$\theta=180^{\circ}$$, so Potential energy $$=p \mathrm{E}$$
